FT_IC1.evidence: Paper analyzes data from ABCD Study Data Release 3.0, 11,878 participants at baseline ages 9-10 years and 6,571 at 2-year follow-up

FT_IC5.evidence: Reports original empirical analysis: bivariate multiple indicator latent change score models examining longitudinal relationships between lifestyle risk factors and brain structure

FT_IC6.evidence: Published peer-reviewed article in Health Psychology, 2023

FT_IC2.evidence: Screen time is part of composite 'lifestyle risk factor domain' bundling sleep, physical activity, BMI, and screen time with no per-modality breakdown. Composite operationalized as single latent factor; no separate phone/SM variable as IV or DV. Per criteria: composite bundling TV/console/phone with no breakdown isolating phone or SM does not qualify

notes: Excluded under FT-EC2 (no qualifying smartphone/SM variable)

FT_IC1.evidence: Participants from ABCD study, ages 9-12 years, first two years of study

FT_IC5.evidence: Reports original empirical analysis with generalized additive mixed models examining self-reported screen media activity related to functional brain organization (resting state fMRI)

FT_IC2.evidence: Screen media activity (SMA) operationalized as composite bundling TV, videos, video games, social media, and other screen activities with no per-modality breakdown isolating phone or SM. Methods: 'average amount of recreational screen time' from Youth Screen Time Survey encompasses multiple modalities. Per criteria: multi-device/multi-activity composite with no breakdown fails FT-IC2

notes: Excluded under FT-EC2 (composite screen time with no phone/SM isolation)

FT_IC1.evidence: Cross-sectional study using ABCD Study data, 21 sites across US, ages 10-14 years, collected September 2018 to September 2020

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis reporting risk ratios and regression models examining associations between screen time/physical activity categories and BMI percentile

FT_IC6.evidence: Published peer-reviewed article in JAMA Network Open, 2023

FT_IC2.evidence: Screen time measured as composite 'recreational screen time' bundling television, videos, video games, texting, social media, and video chat with no per-modality breakdown. Methods: 'hours per day were spent engaging with several different types of media, including television shows and movies, videos, video games...texting, social media, and video chat.' Other side is BMI percentile (demographic/biological measure, not individual-trait-level behavioral/health/cognitive outcome). Per criteria: composite screen time without phone/SM isolation and pairing with only demographic variable fails FT-IC2

notes: Excluded under FT-EC2 (composite screen time bundling multiple modalities; outcome is BMI percentile, a demographic measure)
